sentences.text: Yes, I, I, I can visualize the room that we were in.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were told that we wanted to make it appear as if nobody was in the house.
So it was a kind of a-- kind of thing that we had in the blackout in England, where heavy curtains were drawn at all times, daytime, daytime as well as night.
And, and the minimum of, of lights were, were, were used.
And then, in case that anybody came through the gate, in case they rammed through the gate-- we didn't know what, what means they might use if they did come-- we were told we're to go in the cellar.
There was sort of a hidey place, although it wouldn't have hidden us for long.
But it was better than nothing.
And so all these things when you sort of arrive, they sort of terrorized, traumatized, combination of both.
And you listen very attentively so that you know what to do if they come.
Well, they didn't come.
So things get progressively less tense with every hour that they don't come, obviously.
But it was not a pleasant experience.
They had guest bedrooms.
And it was a very large house.
You know, we must have, to some extent.
But I don't remember it.
And even as I say we must have, maybe we didn't.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But I [LAUGHS] can only repeat we must have.
I can't imagine that we didn't.
But I can't-- in all frankness, I cannot-- I cannot say that I recall having conversations about this with either my mother or my father.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We discussed current events, things as they were happening.
We discussed the situation, the political situation.
We read the papers.
We knew, as I've said many times, what was going on.
We were treated as grown-up beyond our years under the circumstances, and rightly so.
What you're asking really about fears, about feelings.
And that's the part that I really don't recall specifically.
I do not remember them crying at any time.
There was definitely a tradition of stiff upper lip in the family.
There was the, the word 'tapfer,' which is brave, I recall, as being a key word in my childhood.
Something to aim for-- to be not in the-- like the-- like the German-- Germanic heroes [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like Siegfried, et cetera, not in that sense.
Not in a flamboyant way, but tough in the sense of, we don't show the oppressor that you are afraid-- and-- in that sense.
During that time there was no communication, no.
I don't know.
This was between the adults.
I don't know.
My mother got a signal by phone, probably.
He probably knew where she was, where we were.
And she didn't know where he was.
But he, he told her afterwards, but, but not--
It was sort of like Bethesda.
A suburb, yes, yes.
It was near the Olympic stadium, which is a bit outside of the-- it certainly wasn't in the center of the city.
Berlin is built in a sort of a-- there's the inner city, and then there sort of belts of more or less residential and commercial areas as you go toward the outside.
There's not really the, the, the suburbs, as such, what one-- what we would call suburbs aren't really-- they are much more-- much further away.
And they don't-- this belonged to Berlin.
But it had the postal district of Charlottenburg and others would have another name.
But always Berlin hyphen or whatever the district was.
Walked.
We walked.
We walked.
It was not a long walk between the other house and this house.
I believe I, I-- when we were changing tapes, I was talking about the-- in answer to your question about other causes of fear, if I may just complete that--
--that story.
The, the stormtroopers would, would roam the streets.
When I say roam, it wasn't anything random.
It was very military-type marching.
And they carried this Blood Flag.
And the-- every passerby, everybody on the street, had to stand at attention and give the Hitler salute.
And [LAUGHS] I wasn't going to be caught dead giving this Hitler salute.
If you didn't give the Hitler salute, there were a couple of bullies-- or more than a couple-- marching at the side of the corps looking out that everybody really did salute.
And anybody that didn't got beaten up, and depending on their mood, either badly or lightly or just given a kick in the behind, or whatever.
Whatever their mood was, they were the boss.
Well, I didn't want to give the salute.
And I also didn't want to get beaten up.
So I became a master at discovering doorways, hidey-holes of that kind when I saw them coming.
And they were pretty noisy.

They were either singing their party songs and-- or they were-- well, one of the most popular party songs was, 'When Jewish blood splashes from the knife'-- I'm giving you word-by-word translation. 'When Jewish blood splashes from the knife, things go twice as well'-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] just a little illustration.
The-- I, I became an expert in finding places to get away from the street and the, the stare and the examine of these bully types of the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] custodians of the salute.
And I, I didn't-- just once, I, I, I ran-- that one time I ran.
They, they-- I didn't get there in time.
And they started to come after me.
And I-- and I ran.
Phwt!
I ran into a side street.
And they-- by the time I got to the end of the side street, they'd, they'd-- I, I think I could run faster.
They had boots on and all the other crap that they wear.
And they couldn't get too far away from the-- from the corps.
They had, had other jobs to do, i.e., beat other people up.
Remember that I went to England almost exactly one month-- or exactly one month after Kristallnacht, which was 10th of November.
I went to-- I left on 10th of December.
So the main ones that I was aware of were the ones I've mentioned, summarized about doctors and lawyers.
The ones about shops didn't date from that time.
They, they dated from long before.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were-- must have been many others which had been accumulating ever since the end of the Olympic Games.
According to people that have studied the subject, there were-- there were many governing all aspects of daily life.
But they didn't affect us directly apart from this kind of business of the stormtroopers, and so on.
But the regulations, per se, didn't affect us that I knew of.
Maybe my father knew about them.
Maybe the-- my mother knew about them.
I didn't know about them.
I never wore-- no, that was not-- that regulation didn't come into force until after the war, or just before the war started.
I think after the war-- after the war started-- I'm sorry, no, not after the war.
After the war started, which was September, less than a year later-- September-- 2nd of September, '39.
So I never wore the star.
I, I had-- I suddenly remember the time that I got my passport.
It was a children's passport, a one-page thing, not the-- not the book-type passport that we know that adults got.
But I got a, a children's pass, as they called them.
And it had my photo on it and the vital-- the vital statistics.
And I was 14 at the time.
And I think you're considered legally a child until you're 16.

And it had a big J stamped over and on top of it, so that-- And with that, armed with that, one suitcase, and the limit, which was 10 marks-- 10, 10 Reichsmarks-- not deutsche marks, that was before the currency reform, which came after the war-- I set off for England.
But that's the next part of the story.
Right.
No, I was already-- at that time, my father had apparently already made the first-- taken the first steps toward my, my emigration.
And I actually had to go to several, several offices.
I remember going to the Swedish Embassy.
There was some talk about sending me to Sweden rather than England.
And that was apparently still possible.
A friend of mine was, was indeed sent to Sweden, even though his parents came to England in-- sometime in '39, before the war started.
And I went and spent a lot of time in the-- on these formalities, like the passport.
And there were other types of documents that I needed.
And so my, my father decided-- and, and Leonore Goldschmidt was a-- was a friend as well as the director of the school.
And she was-- one of her interests was psychoanalysis, just purely as a-- as a-- as a-- as an interest, not professionally.
She was, I think, in fact, in one or two of the-- of the working groups that my father had on various aspects of, of therapy.
And, and she was-- she, she was consulted on this by-- she was a much more practical person than my father was.
And he consulted her whenever he could, and some other friends that were more practical.
He realized that he was a bit ill-suited for this kind of thing.
And the advice he got was to leave me out of school.
At this point, it would only confuse things.
And I would have more time without school to do this.
It was a month.
It was four weeks-- less than four weeks, actually.
So I didn't mind at all.
Very matter of fact.
It was introduced to me after the Kristallnacht.
I did not know until then that my father had been active and, and looking for ways to get me out of the country.
I say to get 'me' out of the country because my sister was due to do her Abitur, which is the school closing exam, sort of like the French baccalaureate the following year.
They were not trying to get her out of the country.
And that's the kind of thinking for which you have to be German to, to understand.
And I wasn't sufficiently German [LAUGHS] to understand that.
Even then, I sort of lifted an eyebrow.
But you couldn't argue with your parents.
They knew best.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] but and of course, that was the reason why she perished along with, with them because it was much too late.
And I don't think she ever took her Abitur.
But it was unthinkable for a bourgeois family to, to stop their daughter-- stop their-- for that bourgeois family, anyway.

They were trying very hard at the same time, even though it was late in the day-- better late than never-- and they tried very hard to-- for themselves also, and, and my sister, to get out right until the end, until war broke out, even, even afterwards while America was still neutral.

And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and of course, no one-- I dare say no one at that time had an inkling of what the Holocaust would really consists of, what it would cover, and what it would-- what it would be.
I mean, this was something that was unknown in the annals of civilization.
[DRINKS]

That, that, that, I dare say, I have yet, so many years after the war, to find anybody that says, I knew or he knew or they knew, or to show me where it was written, or who said that, that-- who could predict this.

This just wasn't-- so our-- I'm saying this in order to, to, to somehow convey that this was not unwarranted optimism to really think that there would be-- that we would be reunited, that this would be a temporary, rather than a permanent, separation.
And that's, that's the way that, that, that the whole thing was approached.
How much of this was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] kind of-- I can't answer for my parents.
I can't answer for my sister.
For me, I had an inkling that somehow, I can't help thinking was stronger than they.
Anyway, I had a strong inkling that this was not that temporary, that this might well be permanent.
I didn't know how, obviously.
But I was-- I had a lot of trepidation about this.
But there was-- since I couldn't-- I couldn't detail it or document it in any way, there was no way I could put it into words.
There was a feeling.
And my father said, this was going to be temporary.
Great.
I hope he's right, and I'm wrong.

So-- and the one thing, of course, that was totally positive was the getting out of that-- of that hell, the getting out of that life, especially, of course, when you could hold yourself-- hold to yourself the fantasy and the-- and the target and the aim of still being reunited at a later date.
That would be temporary.
Then the whole thing became very, very positive.

And it was only then that one realized, even I-- I'd been in it, but as a child-- and I think a lot of the other children on the-- on the train were in the same boat-- they realized just how badly this had affected them because so much of it was up here.
[POINTS] I don't think many of them had lost-- had been wounded physically.
They'd be beaten, yes.
They'd been-- but they were not seriously harmed physically in that sense.

But I think that they learned on that train, as we were crossing the frontier, that they had been hurt in ways that were just as important or maybe more important because the physical heals and mental, the, the, the psyche, doesn't heal that fast.
So we realized what a hell it was.
Because while you're in it, you sort of always try to look on the bright side.
Yes, but I can still go to the theater.
Yes, but I can still go to that restaurant, you know.
Maybe there is one that does still admit Jews.
Maybe there, there was--
After Kristallnacht, you couldn't rationalize those things anymore.
It was just pure hell, just pure hell, from that point of view, not loss of life yet, but pure hell.
Relief, relief.
It was presented immediately as a temporary separation.
And the idea that-- and my father was-- did this very well.
Well, he was a-- he was a psychoanalyst, [LAUGHS] and he knew me.
And he knew that I was very much, probably in the sense of pride, in the sense of pride of person, I was suffering very deeply from, from this club that was always poised over me.

And and it was-- even though my way out, the way-- the only way that I could, until then, think of was, was, was Palestine, Zionism, even though that was so opposed to what he had believed in all these many years, he still recognized my thinking and respected it for what it was.
And so I think he had a very-- but he knew also that, of course, the separation was the one fly in the ointment, and-- from them, from the family.
And so he, he somehow knew to present it in such a way that the separation would be temporary, the reuniting would be-- would happen.
That came first.
And the, the, the, the, the getting out of hell came second.

So if it had been the other way around, the getting out of hell would have been-- the joy of that would have been ruined by-- might have been ruined by, my worry about, yes, but no, I won't go if I have to leave you behind, et cetera, et cetera.
So there was no-- there was none of that, in that sense.
I did not resist.
No, no, no, no.
I did  not resist.
I, I, I had all kinds of-- kinds of objections.
And certainly, I did say these things.
What if this doesn't happen, if you can't get out, et cetera?
And--
But I didn't know everything that they were doing.
So when he said, we're doing a lot of things.
We're going to get out.
And one of them is going to come off.
You know, he was a very convincing person.

And, in the same way that I respected him as a person, his intellect, his, his power of, of, of thought, I, I, I had to respect what he was saying because this was serious-- this was serious talk about serious matters.
Yes.
Yes, indeed.
Not really, no.
I had some idea that this was being organized by Jewish committees in England and that, somehow, we would be taken care of-- the children would be taken care of.
And it was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] England was not that much of an unknown to me.
I should say very quickly that at the Goldschmidt Schule-- School, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Mrs.

Goldschmidt had, had the foresight-- in '36 I think, already, maybe early '37-- to make contact with the Cambridge University in England and to convince them to appoint her school an examination center of the university in Germany, the first, first center.
They must have thought she was crazy at first, as [LAUGHS] traditional and conservative as the British are, especially the-- especially at Oxford.
And-- but there she is.
She talked them around to it.
And the result was that the most important subjects were taught both in German and in English so that I had fairly fluent, fluent English by the time I got there.
Strong accent, I suppose, somewhat, but I, I, I wasn't as lost as somebody that wouldn't know English.
And when you learn about English history and English things, life, et cetera, you know a little bit about the country.
So-- and I'd always had a very great respect for it and, and interest in it.
So much of the literature that I read had been originally-- well, when I read it before I started to learn English, I read it in German.
There were translations, and, and-- whether they came from English or American authors.
And so it, it, it meant something positive to me.
But in any case, the main thing at that particular time was that I was going from a bloody dictatorship and from oppression, which it was, to a free country.
That was the-- oppression and freedom that was really the--
Yes, I knew that pretty well from the beginning.
Yes.
Yes, I can.

The-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my-- the only one from my family who could take me to the station was my father because my mother had been very, very ill with asthma for some weeks before, had been in hospital for several weeks, was recuperating at the house of a friend.
We didn't-- we had a small house at that point that-- we, we didn't-- we didn't have the resources to, to, to-- I mean, we didn't have the staff, and so on.
This friend had both space and somebody to look after her.
So I said goodbye to her there.
And she was convalescing.
She was getting better, getting stronger.
But there's no question that she couldn't get out of bed and come to the station, you know.
My sister was ill with something less grave.
She was-- she was-- there was some, some valid reason why she couldn't go.
So we had a rather tearful-- both, both farewells were tearful.
But-- then my father took me to the station.
And I have to say, unfortunately, in light of what happened, it was a, a very-- 'we're both men, aren't we?' type of farewell.

And while it was maybe satisfying at that time, in the light of what happened, again, in retrospect, I would rather it had been a more emotional in which we-- scene in which we told each other something of our emotions.
There was very little of that that I recall.
He was full of good advice.
He was warm.
He was-- he was very forthcoming and very positive.
And-- but there wasn't-- it wasn't a terribly emotional scene-- openly emotional scene.
The emotions were there.
But they were clearly under control.
And-- in many of the parents, families that I saw there putting children of theirs on the train, there were quite a few that exercised the same kind of self-control.
I don't know what scenes they  had had at home.
It wasn't-- I didn't-- I wasn't observing, I was too busy [LAUGHS] with my own farewells.
I wasn't-- so I can't really say what the scene was, but it was obviously, openly or controlledly, it was a very emotional scene before the train finally steamed away.
Of course, we went into a train that had some young people, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] adults, that were Jewish, and that would take care of us, look after us, till we got where we were going.
And-- but they were authority figures, you know.
They were not really close.
They sort of told us where to go.
And then, of course, there were the guards, the train guards, which had the, the-- they didn't have the SS uniform.
That didn't come into play until the border station.
And there it did come into play, quite brutally.
Yes.
Any of them.
--my mother and my sister.
Yeah.
[SIGHS] Inklings aren't thoughts.
Maybe they are.
If they are, yes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It goes up and down.
Hope goes up and down.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Until the war, I was in regular correspondence with them through the regular mail.
And after that, it became much more-- much more rare and much more limited, our communication.

But during the war-- or during the, the period before the war, after my departure, before the outbreak of war, they were doing their best to talk to me about-- write to me about the little things of life, about the way that things were, toning down the bad things, playing up the pleasant things-- and always imbued with this hope and giving reports on what, what, what they were pursuing-- what route they were pursuing for immigration.
And so there was always an element of hope.
But that doesn't mean that I was thinking at all times that that would succeed.
No, it was a mix.
No, not quite.
I was on a visit-- on a three-week visit from Berlin, I guess it was, to my uncle, who lived in Frankfurt, in '36 or '37.
On that, I went alone-- and my uncle and his wife.
